title: DATA-DRIVEN EVALUATION OF TRAINING ACTION SPACE FOR REINFORCEMENT LEARNING [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## DATA-DRIVEN EVALUATION OF TRAINING ACTION SPACE FOR REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Training action space selection for reinforcement learning (RL) is conflict-prone due to complex state-action relationships. To address this challenge, this paper proposes a Shapley-inspired methodology for training action space categorization and ranking. To reduce exponential-time shapley computations, the methodology includes a Monte Carlo simulation to avoid unnecessary explorations. The effectiveness of the methodology is illustrated using a cloud infrastructure resource tuning case study. It reduces the search space by 80% and categorizes the training action sets into dispensable and indispensable groups. Additionally, it ranks different training actions to facilitate high-performance yet cost-efficient RL model design. The proposed data-driven methodology is extensible to different domains, use cases, and reinforcement learning algorithms.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

A reinforcement learning (RL) agent learns how to map states to actions in order to maximize a long-term cumulative award signal in a given environment. Figure 1 shows the various artifacts of an RL algorithm. An RL problem is defined by a quartet of ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ), where _S_ is a set of states or the state space; _A_ is a set of actions or the action space available to influence _S_ ; _Pa_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) = _Pr_ ( _st_ +1 = _s[′] |st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) is the transition probability which is the probability that action _a_ in state _s_ at time _t_ will lead to state _s[′]_ at time _t_ + 1; and finally, _Ra_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) is the immediate reward signal received after transitioning from state _s_ to state _s[′]_ , due to action _a_ . An RL agent training involves recognizing an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ : _a ← s_ , from a corpus of _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ to maximize the long-term cumulative reward,[�] _Ra_ . The reward function, _Ra_ , is defined ab initio for an efficient goal accomplishment. The transition probability or state-action mapping is defined by the environment dynamics. In many real-life use cases, the agent cannot directly sense the effect of its actions on the environment. This is particularly true when the state-action relationship cannot be modeled by either closed-form analytical expressions [26, 29, 58] or explicit rules as in the popular games such as Chess [28], Go [47, 46], and Atari [34]. This challenge is well documented in the literature [51, 57]. To address this challenge for RL model training [52, 33], simulation-based action models [50] play a pivotal role. The optimal choice of simulation parameters such as the training action space is a non-trivial challenge because of the curse of dimensionality [17], non-linearity [11], and non-uniform action set [25].

Training data valuation [22, 10] and associated artisanal software engineering efforts constitute a large part of the machine learning (ML) life cycle (or MLOps). Yet, most research and development efforts [36] focus on algorithms and infrastructure. Production-grade MLOps needs to handle data lifecycle management (DLM) [40] challenges including: fairness and bias in labeled datasets [16], data quality [9], limitations of benchmarks [42], and reproducibility concerns [39]. For RL, the DLM challenges are further compounded due to complexities arising from non-linear state-action interactions, partiallyobservable processes, non-isometric action spaces [32], and strong domain specificity [21] of action models. With the recent emphasis on ML explainability, traditional supervised learning and deep learning research communities are actively working on systematic data-driven frameworks [14, 53] for training data valuation. We need equivalent frameworks for RL to streamline conflict-prone training action selection [8, 37, 18]. Depending on the agent-environment interactions, different training actions have different relative contributions to the RL agent performance. Some training actions are indispensable because of their unique positions in the parametric space. Other dispensable actions have different relative contributions to the reward function. _Remarkably, a high-cardinality_

1

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 1: The schematic diagram for a typical reinforcement learning algorithm

_training action space in many cases leads to lower cumulative reward than a well-designed training action space._ To the best of authors’ knowledge, there is hardly any data-driven tool for the evaluation of training action space for RL. Such a tool leads to superior RL agent performance, lower model training and maintenance cost, and strong multi-disciplinary collaboration [35].

This paper proposes a shapley-inspired [54] algorithm to categorize and rank training action sets. It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity. While Section 2 describes the algorithms for RL training action space evaluation, Section 3 illustrates the effectiveness of the algorithms in a specific case study. Section 4 discusses the relevant work. Finally, Section 5 presents a summary with possible future directions.

## 2 ALGORITHM

The proposed framework provides a shapley-inspired methodology [49] to efficiently filter out unnecessary training actions and discover a near-optimal action set. Figure 2 shows the framework consists of two algorithms and how these two algorithms interact with each other to design a nearoptimal RL model by training action space filtering and evaluation. The first algorithm computes cutoff cardinality for the training action space. The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound. The second algorithm takes action points only above the cut-off cardinality and categorize action points into two classes: _{dispensable, indispensable}_ and ranking dispensable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards. Indispensable action points are absolute essential action points for the RL training to achieve the desired goal. On the other hand, dispensable action points are non-essential action points that can be discarded. However, the RL agent might perform sub-optimally without a disposable action point. By ranking disposable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards, the relative contributions of different training action points are estimated.

## 2.1 CUT-OFF CARDINALITY COMPUTATION FOR TRAINING ACTION POINTS

RL deals with compilation of a quartet ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) and computation of an optimal policy, _π[∗]_ which maximizes the long-term cumulative reward. The search space for a shapley-inspired technique needs to span across the power set for the training action space which has exponential complexity with training action points. To circumvent this problem, the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality. The inputs to this algorithm include an RL algorithm, the power set for the training action space, the corresponding state space,

2

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

Figure 2: The proposed training action selection framework for reinforcement learning

the transition probability, an acceptable performance margin, and an acceptable number of iterations. The output is the cut-off cardinality. The algorithm uses an iterative procedure to train the RL agent with different numbers of training actions, starting from all available training actions to only one training action. If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one. The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cadinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.

**Algorithm 1** Monte Carlo method for cut-off cardinality computation for the training action space **Input:** The RL algorithm, _R_ , the power set for the training action space, _P_ ( _A_ ), the corresponding state space, _P_ ( _S|A_ ), transition probability _**P**_ , an acceptable performance margin, _ϵ_ , and an acceptable number of iterations, _n_ . **Output:** Cut-off cardinality, _|A|cutoff_ **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted. **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1 **end if end for**

## 2.2 CATEGORIZATION AND RANKING OF TRAINING ACTIONS

Algorithm 2 offers a principled procedure for training action selection. As for inputs, it takes the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality, determined by Algorithm 1, the RL algorithm, the reward function, the threshold condition, and the acceptable number of iterations. As for outputs, it predicts a categorization vector which classifies training action points into two classes: { _dispensable_ , _indispensable_ } and ranks dispensable training actions based on the corresponding cumulative reward. At its core, Algorithm 2 determines whether the policy determined from the given state-action pairs achieves the threshold condition within the acceptable number of iterations. The algorithm uncovers the indispensability of some training action sets for a given RL task. A training action set is deemed to be indispensable: _iff_ upon removal of that training action

3

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

point, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal within a finite number of iterations [24, 56, 23]. All other action points are dispensable. The dispensable action points are rank listed in _L_ based on the corresponding cumulative reward. Essentially, _L_ informs the performance impact of different disposable action points on the RL agent.

**Algorithm 2** Data-driven categorization and ranking for training action space evaluation

**Input:** All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ , the RL algorithm, _R_ , the reward function, the threshold condition, and the acceptable number of iterations. **Output:** Categorization vector, _V_ , which classifies training action points into two classes: { _dispensable_ , _indispensable_ }. Rank order list of dispensable training actions based on the corresponding cumulative reward.

**repeat for** _< si, ai >_ **do** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ )

**if** Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied _within_ the acceptable number of iterations **then** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_

**else** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← dispensable_ **end if end for until** all necessary training action sets are evaluated. Rank List, _L ←_ dispensable actions sorted in order of the corresponding cumulative rewards

## 2.3 ACTION SHAPLEY

The proposed framework adopts Action Shapley which introduces a formulation for the problem of equitable training action valuation in reinforcement learning. Reinforcement learning has three key building blocks. The first building block is the training action space, _A_ , the corresponding state space _S_ , and a pre-assigned reward function, _Ra_ . The second building block is a reinforcement learning algorithm, _R_ , which is treated as a black box. It takes ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) and computes an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ . The third building block is the performance measure or the cumulative sum of reward,[�] _Ra_ . The goal of the framework is to compute the valuation for each training action point, _φi_ ( _A, R,_[�] _Ra_ ) as a function of three building blocks. A shapley technique is useful for action valuation because it satisfies nullity, symmetry, and linearity [45]. From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:

where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant. We call _φi_ the Action Shapley value of action _ai_ . Equation 1 suggests Action Shapley computation is with exponential time complexity w.r.t. the training action points. This exponential computational complexity warrants the need for the cut-off cardinality discussed in Algorithm 1.

## 3 CASE STUDY

In this section, we design a resource tuning example (in the cloud) to illustrate the effectiveness of the proposed algorithm for the training action space evaluation. We evaluate the performance of an MDP [20]-based RL agent, as shown in Figure 1. The state-action mapping and transition probability are modeled using time-series auto-regression [38] after a PCA-based dimensionality reduction [41] with time complexity _O_ ( _klogk_ ), where _k_ is the number of principal components. The choice of the algorithms is purely driven by the nature of the training dataset. A more complex non-linear dataset warrants more complex sequential models such as long short-term memory (LSTM) [44] or attention based models [30]. For action updates, WLOG, an RL-based PID controller [1, 13] is used. Similar to time-series modeling, action update can be conducted by other policy learning algorithms such as SARSA [19].

Table 1: Relevant case study for cloud resource tuning

|**RL Artifact**|**Description**|
|---|---|
|_State_|CPU utilization metrics|
|_State Statistics_|Median value of CPU utilization|
|_Threshold_|90% of CPU utilization|
|_Action_|Resource confguration set points: (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB))|
|_Reward_|Negative of total time steps required to satisfy the threshold condition|
|_Parametric Boundary_|Polygon defned by the parametric endpoints|
|_Initial Action_|(6_,_14): Arbitrarily assigned WLOG|
|_Error Margin_|5%|
|_Acceptable Steps_|400|
|_Computational Complexity_|Polynomial time|

Table 2: Five different AWS EC2 resource pairs used in training action space

|**EC2 Type**|**of vCPUs**|**Memory Size(GB)**|
|---|---|---|
|_small t3a_|2|2|
|_medium t3a_|2|4|
|_large t3a_|2|8|
|_xlarge t3a_|4|16|
|_2xlarge t3a_|8|32|

As shown in Table 1, the objective of the RL agent for this case study is to quickly reduce high CPU utilization below a pre-assigned threshold for a given workload. In most infrastructure/cloud resource tuning technologies [55], CPU utilization represents a key metric. Therefore, the state space for this RL case study consists of virtual machine CPU utilization (%) metrics and the action space is defined by the VM resource set points, i.e., (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)). The RL agent uses AWS boto3 SDK [2] to manipulate actions and AWS CloudWatch [3] for state space monitoring. The reward is defined as the number of time steps required by the agent to accomplish the objective multiplied by _−_ 1. The negative reward per time step was meant to push the agent to accomplish the task as fast as possible.

The training data for this case study was generated internally [4] with an open source library, _stress_ (https://linux.die.net/man/1/stress). It uses a rectangular workload. The peak of the workload uses the stress command: _sudo stress –io 4 –vm 2 –vm-bytes 1024M –timeout 500s_ . Essentially, the peak is running 4 _I/O_ stressors and 2 VM workers spinning on malloc with 1024 MB per worker for 500 s. The simulated rectangular workload has a time period of 600 s: a high-stress phase of 500 s is followed by an inactive phase of 100 s. The RL training action space is spanned by the power set drawn from the five pairs of EC2 configuration set points as shown in Table 2. Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space. For each EC2 instance in the training action space, the corresponding state metrics, i.e., CPU utilization (%) are shown in Figure 3. The training data was collected for a 24 hour period with 1 minute sampling interval. Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.

With this set up, different RL models are developed with different training action sets and the corresponding rewards and ranks are noted in Table 4. Remarkably, a high cardinality training action set does not guarantee the best reward: the agent with all training actions, _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , does not yield the highest reward. In fact, the action set with _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward. This pattern could be attributed to the state-action interaction in this particular case study. First, the parametric distances

|**Training Action Set**|**Reward**|**Rank**|
|---|---|---|
|_<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_|_−_21|3|

|_<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_|_−_16|2|

between different EC2 instance pairs are not uniform. While the Euclidean distance between _small t3a_ and _medium t3a_ is equal to 2, that between _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ is 16 _._ 5. The non-uniform spacing for training action space is a considerable deterrent [31] for RL adoption. Second, in this case study, the transient CPU utilization (%) patterns have undergone a material change from _xlarge t3a_ (max 100%) to _2xlarge t3a_ (max 73%). This indicates the strong influence of _2xlarge t3a_ for the given RL task. Indeed, we noticed _2xlarge t3a_ to be an indispensable training action. As shown in Table 3, a categorization of training actions can be inferred based on the valuation vector, _V_ , as described in Algorithm 2: two _dispensable_ training actions are uncovered to be _small t3a, medium t3a_ and three _indispensable_ training actions to be _large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a_ .

Figure 4 shows the RL loop action with three training actions: _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , and _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ .

- For the first training action set of _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , the reward is _−_ 21. The left subplot in Figure 4(a) shows how the recommended action is evolving with time from an _arbitrary_ initial point of (6, 14). The recommended points are superimposed on the training action parameter space to illustrate their relative position with respect to the parametric boundary which is defined by the trapezium with vertices:

- _{(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)}_ in the (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)) space. The right subplot in Figure 4(a) shows how the median CPU utilization (%) comes below the _90%_ threshold in 21 steps leading to _−_ 21 in reward. The blue dots represent the median CPU utilizations for different training set points.
- For the second training action set of _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , the reward is _−_ 13 as shown in Figure 4(b).
- For the third training action set of _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , the reward is _−_ 16 as shown in Figure 4(c).

As shown in Table 4, the reward scores can indeed be used for ranking different training action sets leading to a data-driven approach for training action selection. As shown in Figure 5, _large t3a_ is an indispensable element in the training action space for the given RL agent. Without this training action, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal of bringing the CPU utilization below the critical threshold. Similar observations can be made about _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ .

.

## 4 RELATED WORK

Shapley value was postulated in a classic paper in game theory [15] and influences the field of economics significantly [43]. Various shapley-inspired techniques have been applied to model diverse problems including voting [12], resource allocation [48] and bargaining [27]. Recent years saw increased application of shapley-inspired methodologies in machine learning feature importance evaluation and training data valuation [14, 10]. To the best of our knowledge, Shapley value has not been used to quantify training action valuation in a reinforcement learning context. The application of shapley-inspired technique for reinforcement learning is a challenging problem. First, the relationship between state-action pairs and transition probabilities are expensive to model. Also, often the RL training action points are not uniformly positioned. Finally, we have purposefully focused on a small sample cloud infrastructure use case instead of cliched RL use cases [5]. That is because the problem of optimal action selection has relatively more expensive in the cloud infrastructure space where the state-action relationship is only partially observable.

## 5 CONCLUSION

This paper proposes a data-driven methodology for training action space evaluation for RL. The methodology offers a principled framework for training action space categorization and ranking within a finite computational time. It unleashes a strategy for superior model performance and lower modeling cost. Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms. Therefore, it is a general-purpose methodology extensible to different domains including distributed computing, network traffic control, healthcare, automatic locomotion, building management system, and industrial controls, and different machine learning algorithms such as PCA, Autoencoder, ARIMA, LSTM, Transformer, PID, SARSA, DQN, and many others. For the next phase of the development for this data-agnostic methodology, we are planning to contribute a general RL design library to relevant open source projects [6, 5, 7].

- [43] Alvin E Roth. _The Shapley value: essays in honor of Lloyd S. Shapley_ . Cambridge University Press, 1988.
- [44] Alaa Sagheer and Mostafa Kotb. “Time series forecasting of petroleum production using deep LSTM recurrent networks”. In: _Neurocomputing_ 323 (2019), pp. 203–213.
- [45] Anthony F Shorrocks. _Decomposition procedures for distributional analysis: a unified framework based on the Shapley value_ . Tech. rep. mimeo, University of Essex, 1999.
- [46] David Silver et al. “Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks and tree search”. In: _nature_ 529.7587 (2016), pp. 484–489.
- [47] Satinder Singh, Andy Okun, and Andrew Jackson. “Learning to play Go from scratch”. In: _Nature_ 550.7676 (2017), pp. 336–337.

- [48] Peibing Song et al. “Comprehensive Benefit Allocation for Joint Operation of Multiple Reservoirs in the Ningbo Based on Shapely-Value Method and Benefit-Incremental Method”. In: _MATEC Web of Conferences_ . Vol. 246. EDP Sciences. 2018, p. 01080.
- [49] Mukund Sundararajan and Amir Najmi. “The many Shapley values for model explanation”. In: _International Conference on Machine Learning_ . PMLR. 2020, pp. 9269–9278.

zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3-flash-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and practical problem: optimizing the training action space in Reinforcement Learning to improve efficiency and performance. It introduces a 'Shapley-inspired' methodology to categorize actions as dispensable or indispensable. While the conceptual motivation is sound and the case study is illustrative, the paper suffers from significant formal gaps. Key definitions (like the 'valuation vector' and 'Action Shapley' in Equation 1) are under-defined or mathematically vague, and Algorithm 2 contains a logical circularity where it attempts to define a vector based on the removal of an action index $i$ that is not explicitly indexed in the loop. Furthermore, the application of Shapley values to RL actions is conceptually complex, yet the paper glosses over how the value function $v(S)$—essential for Shapley calculations—is formally defined in the context of RL training trajectories.

comments.title: Logical circularity or indexing error in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: for $< si, ai >$ do ... if Threshold condition is never satisfied ... then $V(A - ai) \leftarrow indispensable$
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the loop iterates over state-action pairs $<si, ai>$. However, the assignment to the categorization vector $V$ uses the notation $(A - ai)$. This is confusing: usually, $V$ would be indexed by the action being evaluated (e.g., $V_i$). If the intention is to say 'the set $A$ without $a_i$ is indispensable,' it contradicts the standard definition where a single player/action is categorized. Furthermore, the logic seems to imply that if the agent fails *without* an action, that action is indispensable, but the code says $V(A-ai) \leftarrow indispensable$, which suggests the remaining set is indispensable. This is likely an index/notation error that obscures the categorization logic.

comments.title: Imprecise definition of Action Shapley value components
comments.quote: $\phi_{i}(A, R, [\Sigma] R_{a}) = \frac{1}{C} \sum_{S \subseteq A \setminus\{i\}} \frac{|S|!(|A|-|S|-1)!}{|A|!}(v(S \cup\{i\})-v(S))$
comments.explanation: Equation 1 is presented as the core 'Action Shapley' formula. However, the term $v(S)$ is never formally defined in the text. In standard Shapley theory, $v(S)$ is the characteristic function mapping a subset of players to a real value. Here, the 'players' are training actions. It is unclear if $v(S)$ refers to the cumulative reward achieved by an agent trained only on action subset $S$, the probability of convergence, or some other metric. Without defining $v(S)$, the formula is not reproducible. Additionally, the constant $C$ is left as an 'arbitrary constant,' which is unconventional for Shapley values where $C$ is typically 1 to satisfy the efficiency axiom.

comments.title: Inconsistency in Algorithm 1 termination and output
comments.quote: if all statuses for a given cardinality, $k$, are failures then $|A|cutoff \leftarrow k + 1$
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 iterates from $k = |A|$ down to 1. The goal is to find the minimum cardinality. However, the logic states that if *all* sets of size $k$ fail, the cutoff is $k+1$. This implies that the algorithm should stop as soon as it finds a $k$ that fails. However, the 'for' loop continues. If a later (smaller) $k$ also fails, the $|A|cutoff$ would be overwritten. While a reader can infer the 'lowest successful k' is intended, as written, the algorithm is underspecified regarding its exit condition and handling of the $k$ loop.

comments.title: Unjustified reduction of search space claim
comments.quote: It reduces the search space by 80% and categorizes the training action sets into dispensable and indispensable groups.
comments.explanation: The authors claim an 80% reduction in search space (specifically 81.25% in the case study). This number is derived by manually focusing on action sets above a cardinality of 4. However, the 'search space' for Shapley values is the power set. Calculating the cutoff cardinality itself requires running the RL agent for various combinations (Algorithm 1). The paper does not provide a complexity analysis to prove that the overhead of finding the cutoff is significantly less than the original problem, especially since the 'Monte Carlo' aspect mentioned in the abstract is not rigorously detailed in Algorithm 1 beyond standard iteration.

comments.title: Ambiguous status of Algorithm reference
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ?? , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 . 1
comments.explanation: The text contains a LaTeX compilation error (??) where a specific algorithm or section was supposed to be referenced. This makes it difficult to verify how the 'optimal step-size' was determined, as neither Algorithm 1 nor Algorithm 2 explicitly handles step-size optimization for action updates; they focus on action space cardinality and categorization.

zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Zero Shot (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
zero_shot__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a novel and timely framework for data-driven evaluation of training action spaces in reinforcement learning, leveraging Shapley-inspired methods to categorize and rank actions. The motivation is strong, particularly in highlighting the underexplored problem of action space valuation, and the case study in cloud resource tuning provides a concrete and relevant application. The overall structure is logical, and the integration of Monte Carlo simulation to address computational complexity is a reasonable design choice. However, several technical and logical issues—particularly in algorithm specification, notation consistency, and methodological underspecification—undermine the reproducibility and clarity of the approach. While the core idea is promising, key parts of the methodology lack sufficient detail or contain inconsistencies that prevent full validation of the claims.

comments.title: Ambiguous and incorrect logic in cut-off cardinality algorithm
comments.quote: **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted. **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1 **end if end for**
comments.explanation: The logic of Algorithm 1 is problematic. It iterates from |A| down to 1, training the RL agent at each cardinality k, but the condition for setting the cut-off is that all statuses at cardinality k are 'failures', then setting |A|cutoff ← k+1. However, if k decreases, and failures occur at smaller k, this could set the cut-off higher than |A|, which is nonsensical. Worse, the algorithm trains with 'different numbers of training actions' but does not specify how subsets are sampled at each k. The phrase 'all necessary training action sets' is vague—does it mean all subsets of size k? That would still be exponential. More critically, the algorithm claims to avoid exponential complexity, yet evaluating 'all' subsets at any k remains combinatorial. The Monte Carlo method is mentioned in the text but not reflected in the pseudocode. The pseudocode appears deterministic and exhaustive. The intention seems to be sampling subsets at each k, but this is not specified. This omission makes the algorithm underspecified and potentially misaligned with the paper’s stated goal of polynomial complexity.

comments.title: Incorrect input to Algorithm 1: power set of state space conditional on action
comments.quote: the power set for the training action space, _P_ ( _A_ ), the corresponding state space, _P_ ( _S|A_ )
comments.explanation: The input _P_ ( _S|A_ ) is ill-defined. The power set notation _P_ usually denotes the set of all subsets, but _S|A_ implies a conditional, which is inconsistent. There is no standard meaning for the power set of a conditional space. The authors likely mean the state space induced by or associated with A, but expressing it as _P(S|A)_ suggests a probabilistic dependency, which clashes with set-theoretic notation. This notation is not clarified elsewhere. Given that _S_ and _A_ are sets in the MDP definition, _P(S|A)_ appears to be a notational error—either they mean the state space corresponding to A (e.g., _S_A_), or they misunderstand the meaning of _P_ as probability rather than power set. The confusion risks misinterpretation of the algorithm’s inputs. While _P(A)_ could plausibly mean the power set of A, _P(S|A)_ cannot be interpreted analogously without redefinition.

comments.title: Misuse of valuation vector indexing in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: **if** Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied _within_ the acceptable number of iterations **then** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_ **else** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← dispensable_
comments.explanation: The valuation vector _V_ is indexed by _**A** − ai_, which is a set, not a single action. However, the output is supposed to classify individual training action points. The condition checks whether removing _ai_ causes failure, so the label should be assigned to _ai_, not to the set _A − ai_. The correct assignment should be _V(ai) ← indispensable_. The current form suggests _V_ maps sets to labels, contradicting the stated output 'categorization vector' which classifies individual actions. This is a serious indexing error that misrepresents the algorithm’s logic. The surrounding text confirms that actions are classified individually (e.g., Table 3), so the pseudocode contradicts the intended behavior.

comments.title: Underspecified Monte Carlo implementation and sampling strategy
comments.quote: the algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality
comments.explanation: Despite repeated emphasis on using Monte Carlo simulation to avoid exponential computation, no sampling mechanism appears in Algorithm 1. The loop iterates over cardinalities, but how the subsets of size k are selected is not specified. A Monte Carlo approach would require random sampling of subsets at each k, yet the pseudocode implies an exhaustive 'until all necessary... are exhausted' loop, which remains exponential. The number of samples per k, the distribution over subsets, and the stopping criteria for sampling are all missing. Without this, the method cannot be reproduced, and the claim of polynomial complexity is unsubstantiated. The text mentions 'Monte Carlo' but fails to integrate it into the algorithm description, leaving a critical gap.

comments.title: Incorrect Shapley value formula omission
comments.quote:
From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1: 

where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation:
The paper refers to 'Equation 1' but no equation is provided in the text. This is likely a typesetting or OCR issue, but assuming the intended formula is the standard Shapley value, it should be:
_φi(v) = Σ_{S⊆A\{i}} [ |S|!(|A|−|S|−1)! / |A|! ] (v(S∪{i}) − v(S))_.
The paper's description misses the marginal contribution term and the combinatorial weights. Instead, it references only a sum over subsets and an arbitrary constant _C_, which is not standard. While the authors cite [45], the omission of the actual formula and its key components (especially the marginal value difference) renders the definition incomplete and potentially misleading. A careful reader cannot verify correctness without the full expression. Given that the paper builds on this definition, its absence is a substantial flaw.

comments.title: Inconsistent treatment of Action Shapley input parameters
comments.quote: compute the valuation for each training action point, _φi_ ( _A, R,_[] _Ra_ )
comments.explanation: The function _φi_ is defined as taking _A_, _R_, and [] _Ra_, but [] _Ra_ is never defined. Earlier, _Ra_ is the immediate reward, and [] _Ra_ is referred to as the cumulative reward. However, the Shapley value in standard form depends on a value function over coalitions, _v(S)_, which maps subsets of actions to performance (e.g., expected cumulative reward). The paper does not define _v_, nor clarify how _R_ and _Ra_ are used to compute _v(S)_. The expression _φi(A, R, [] Ra)_ suggests the Shapley value depends directly on the RL algorithm and reward function, but in practice, it depends on the performance metric induced by them. This conflation obscures how the valuation is actually computed and risks confusion about the role of the RL algorithm as a black box. The formalism is underspecified.

comments.title: Erroneous justification for cut-off cardinality directionality
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cadinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.
comments.explanation: This claim is backwards. A larger cut-off cardinality means more actions are retained, which increases the size of the action space and thus computational overhead during training. The algorithm sets |A|_cutoff as the minimal number of actions needed for acceptable performance, so a smaller cutoff should correspond to lower overhead. The stated logic contradicts standard complexity intuition. The authors may have confused 'cutoff' with 'reduction': a higher cutoff (in retained actions) leads to higher cost, not lower. This misstatement could mislead readers about the trade-off being optimized. It overstates the efficiency benefit of a 'larger' cutoff, when in fact efficiency improves with smaller action spaces.

comments.title: Underspecified step-size determination in case study
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The reference 'Algorithm **??**' indicates a missing algorithm number, likely due to OCR or formatting. More importantly, the method for identifying the optimal step-size is not described in the paper. Is it grid search? Optimization over a validation set? RL hyperparameter tuning? This step is critical for reproducibility, especially since the action space in the case study is discrete (EC2 types), yet a continuous step-size of 0.1 is used. It’s unclear how this continuous update interacts with the discrete action set. The omission undermines the credibility of the experimental setup and prevents replication.

zero_shot__glm-4.6.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.6)
zero_shot__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: The paper presents an interesting approach to evaluating training action spaces in RL using Shapley values, which addresses an important problem in RL practical applications. The case study on cloud resource tuning provides concrete validation. However, the paper has several significant issues in the algorithmic descriptions that undermine the methodology's clarity and correctness. The main algorithms contain logical flaws and ambiguities that would prevent reproducibility, while some key mathematical content is missing.

comments.title: Logical flaw in cutoff cardinality determination
comments.quote: if all statuses for a given cardinality, k, are failures then _|A|cutoff ← k + 1
comments.explanation: This condition in Algorithm 1 has a fundamental logical flaw. If all action sets of cardinality k fail to achieve the performance threshold, setting the cutoff to k+1 doesn't make mathematical sense. For example, if k=1 (testing single actions) and all fail, the algorithm would set cutoff to 2, but this contradicts the fact that we're testing whether 1 action is sufficient. The correct logic should be: if all sets of size k fail, then we need MORE than k actions, so the algorithm should continue searching for a larger k. However, since the algorithm iterates from |A| down to 1, this creates a contradiction in the search direction.

comments.title: Unclear handling of action combinations in Algorithm 2
comments.quote: Input: All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: {_ (si, ai) _}
comments.explanation: Algorithm 2's input mentions 'combinations' of actions, but the algorithm body only processes individual actions in a loop. It's unclear how combinations of actions are evaluated. Does the algorithm test each combination as a unit, or does it test removing each action from combinations? The notation V(A - ai) suggests evaluating the set without action ai, but this doesn't align with processing combinations. This ambiguity makes the algorithm's behavior unclear and non-reproducible.

comments.title: Missing Action Shapley formula
comments.quote: From game theory, φ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: The paper references Equation 1 for the Shapley value formulation but the equation itself is not provided in the text. This is a significant omission since the Action Shapley value is central to the proposed methodology. Without the actual formula, readers cannot understand or verify the mathematical foundation of the approach.

comments.title: Unspecified algorithm reference for step-size selection
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ??, the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0.1
comments.explanation: The text references 'Algorithm ??' for identifying the optimal step-size but doesn't specify which algorithm this refers to. This could be a placeholder or OCR error, but since no other algorithms are mentioned in this context, it creates ambiguity about how the step-size was determined.

comments.title: Ambiguous condition in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted
comments.explanation: The phrase 'exhausted' is not clearly defined in Algorithm 1. Does it mean all possible combinations of size k have been tested? Or does it mean a certain number of random samples have been tried? This ambiguity affects the algorithm's reproducibility and computational complexity.

comments.title: Incorrect polygon vertex ordering
comments.quote: parametric boundary which is defined by the trapezium with vertices: {(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)}
comments.explanation: The vertices are listed in an order that doesn't form a proper trapezoid when connected sequentially. To form a trapezoid, the vertices should be ordered either clockwise or counterclockwise around the perimeter. The correct order would be {(2,2), (8,2), (8,32), (2,8)} or {(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)} with appropriate connections specified. While readers can infer the intended shape, this is technically incorrect.

comments.title: Vague computational complexity entry
comments.quote: Computational Complexity: Polynomial time
comments.explanation: Table 1 lists 'Computational Complexity: Polynomial time' but doesn't specify what this refers to. Is it the complexity of the RL algorithm, the state-action mapping, or the overall methodology? Without clarification, this entry provides no meaningful information to the reader.

zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Zero Shot (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
zero_shot__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback: The paper presents a novel and potentially useful application of Shapley values for pruning training action spaces in reinforcement learning. The methodology addresses a clear practical need for reducing computational overhead in complex, partially observable environments. However, the paper suffers from significant technical omissions. Most critically, the formal definition of the Action Shapley value is missing a complete expression, and the algorithmic descriptions are underspecified. The link between the theoretical framework and the empirical results in the case study is difficult to verify because crucial procedural details and internal variables are undefined or referred to via broken placeholders.

comments.title: Missing formal definition of Action Shapley value
comments.quote:
From game theory, $\phi $ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:

where, the sum is over all subsets of $A$ not containing $i$ and $C$ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The paper references 'Equation 1' and provides a text description of the sum, but the actual LaTeX block containing the mathematical formula is empty or missing in the source. As this is the core theoretical contribution of the paper, the absence of the formula makes the methodology impossible to reconstruct or evaluate mathematically.

comments.title: Dangling reference to undefined algorithm
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ?? , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 . 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The text references 'Algorithm ??', which is not defined anywhere in the paper. Because the paper relies on specific step-sizes and iterative updates to achieve the results shown in the case study, this omission makes the methodology underspecified and non-reproducible.

comments.title: Algorithmic loops are ill-defined
comments.quote:
repeat for $< s_i, a_i >$ do
$\pi[*] \leftarrow R ( S, A, P_a, R_a )$
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 2, the 'repeat' loop is conceptually unclear. It iterates over '<s_i, a_i>' but the subsequent line implies re-training the entire policy R from scratch. If the loop is over every single state-action pair in the training set (which could be massive), this is computationally infeasible. The criteria for 'until' are also vague, as it says 'all necessary training action sets are evaluated', which does not align with the formal structure of a standard algorithm.

comments.title: Inconsistency in reward notation
comments.quote: maximize the long-term cumulative reward,[] $R_a$ .
comments.explanation: The document contains several instances of a garbled character '[]' appearing before references to $R_a$. Given the context, this appears to be a typesetting or encoding error during OCR processing that interferes with the reading of the objective. It is unclear if $R_a$ is meant to be a sum, an expectation, or something else. I have assumed it is intended to be the sum, but the ambiguity persists.

zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Zero Shot (deepseek-v4-flash)
zero_shot__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: The paper proposes a Shapley-inspired method for action space evaluation in RL, but the actual implementation diverges significantly: it uses a leave-one-out heuristic instead of computing Shapley values, and the crucial Shapley equation is missing from the paper. The algorithms are vaguely described, making reproduction difficult, and the case study is too small to demonstrate generalizability. These gaps undermine the main contributions and claims.

comments.title: Missing Shapley value equation
comments.quote: From game theory, φ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1: where, the sum is over all subsets of A not containing i and C is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The equation itself is missing from the paper; the reader cannot see the formula. This is a critical omission as the entire methodology claims to be Shapley-inspired. The standard Shapley formula is required for understanding the proposed method.

comments.title: Mismatch between claimed Shapley approach and implemented leave-one-out
comments.quote: The proposed framework adopts Action Shapley which introduces a formulation for the problem of equitable training action valuation in reinforcement learning.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to use Shapley values, but Algorithm 2 only performs leave-one-out evaluation (removing one action at a time). The categorization into dispensable/indispensable is determined solely by whether removing a single action causes failure, not by computing Shapley values over subsets. The ranking of dispensable actions is based on the cumulative reward of the set without that action, not on marginal contributions. This is a fundamental departure from the stated Shapley methodology, and the case study never computes Shapley values.

comments.title: Underspecified Monte Carlo algorithm for cut-off cardinality
comments.quote: repeat until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'all necessary training action sets' is undefined. It is unclear how to select which subsets to evaluate. The algorithm also does not specify the performance metric for 'improves by ϵ within n iterations' — improves relative to what? The algorithm iterates from |A| down to 1, but the condition is ambiguous without a baseline. This vagueness makes the algorithm non-reproducible.

comments.title: Inconsistency in usage of cut-off cardinality
comments.quote: it takes the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality
comments.explanation: Section 2.2 states the algorithm uses combinations 'above' the cut-off cardinality, while Algorithm 2's input says 'upto cut-off cardinality'. Additionally, the case study uses combinations of size exactly equal to the cut-off cardinality (4) and above (5), creating confusion about which subsets are included. This inconsistency could mislead a reader.

comments.title: Missing reference for optimal step-size algorithm
comments.quote: Using Algorithm ??, the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0.1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The algorithm referenced (likely for step-size selection) is missing. The reader cannot verify how this step-size was determined, which undermines the reproducibility of the case study.

comments.title: Claim about Shapley properties not substantiated
comments.quote: A shapley technique is useful for action valuation because it satisfies nullity, symmetry, and linearity.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts these properties as motivation for using Shapley, but the actual implemented method (leave-one-out) does not satisfy symmetry or linearity in the same way. The connection is not justified, and the paper does not check these properties in the case study.

comments.title: No computation of Shapley values in case study
comments.quote: Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results.
comments.explanation: The case study never computes the Action Shapley values φ_i. The evaluation relies entirely on leave-one-out classification and ranking. The paper claims to provide a Shapley-inspired methodology, but the experimental validation does not include actual Shapley values, making the claim unsubstantiated by the evaluation.

zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Zero Shot (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
zero_shot__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: The paper addresses a timely and practically relevant problem: pruning reinforcement learning training action spaces using data-driven valuation. The cloud infrastructure case study is well-motivated, and the empirical setup (EC2 instance selection, CPU utilization targets, reward formulation) is clearly described. However, the manuscript contains several critical technical gaps that undermine reproducibility and theoretical clarity. The most pressing issues are the complete omission of Equation 1 (central to the Shapley-inspired claim), contradictory and underspecified algorithmic descriptions in Algorithms 1 and 2, an unresolved placeholder reference, and a mismatch between the prose definition of cutoff cardinality and its algorithmic implementation. Additionally, the paper overstates its connection to Shapley value theory, as the proposed methods rely on threshold-based ablation rather than coalition-wise marginal valuation. Addressing these structural and formal gaps is essential before the methodology can be properly evaluated or reproduced.

comments.title: Missing Equation 1 for Action Shapley
comments.quote:
From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1: 


where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly references Equation 1 and describes its components (sum over subsets, arbitrary constant C), but the equation itself is entirely absent from the manuscript. I initially assumed it might be in an appendix or deferred to a later section, but a thorough scan confirmed it is missing. This is a critical omission for a paper that centers on a 'Shapley-inspired methodology.' Without the explicit formula, readers cannot verify how the authors handle normalization, whether C affects the ranking, or how the exponential complexity is practically mitigated in their framework. The standard Shapley formula is well-established, but its absence here breaks the theoretical link to the claimed axioms (nullity, symmetry, linearity).

comments.title: Contradictory and Underspecified Algorithm 1 (Cut-off Cardinality)
comments.quote: **repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted.
comments.explanation: Section 2.1 claims the algorithm uses a 'Monte Carlo method,' but the loop condition specifies 'until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted,' which implies exhaustive enumeration rather than stochastic sampling. This is a direct contradiction. Furthermore, the procedure for evaluating whether 'the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_' is undefined: improves relative to what baseline? A prior episode? A random baseline? How many episodes constitute 'n iterations' of training vs. evaluation? The algorithm also prescribes retraining _π[∗]_ from scratch for every subset evaluation, which contradicts the stated goal of reducing computational overhead. Without specifying the sampling distribution, evaluation protocol, or improvement metric, the method is unreproducible.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in Algorithm 2 (Categorization)
comments.quote: **Input:** All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ ... **Output:** Categorization vector, _V_, which classifies training action points into two classes... **repeat for** _< si, ai >_ **do** ... **if** Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied ... **then** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_ **else** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← dispensable_
comments.explanation: The input lists 'combinations' (subsets) up to the cut-off cardinality, but the loop iterates over individual pairs _<si, ai>_ and assigns values to _V(A − ai)_. This notation suggests evaluating the full action set minus one element, which contradicts the stated input of evaluating subsets. Additionally, _V_ is described as a vector classifying individual training actions, yet the indexing _V(A − ai)_ treats it as a set-valued mapping. I checked whether _A_ refers to a specific subset from the input or the global action space, but the algorithm does not specify how the baseline set for ablation is constructed. This logical gap makes the categorization step mathematically incoherent and impossible to implement as written.

comments.title: Uncorrected Placeholder 'Algorithm ??' in Case Study
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: Section 3 contains an obvious drafting artifact: 'Algorithm ??'. I checked whether this referred to Algorithm 1 or 2, but neither procedure is designed to tune continuous step-sizes. The step-size identification is a critical hyperparameter choice that directly impacts the RL convergence shown in Figure 4, yet the methodology for selecting 0.1 is completely missing. Readers are left unable to reproduce the action update dynamics or assess whether the reported rewards are sensitive to this unexplained choice.

comments.title: Definition Mismatch: Cut-off Cardinality Prose vs. Algorithm
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound. ... If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: The prose defines cutoff cardinality in terms of 'high-fidelity predictions' and an 'error bound,' but Algorithm 1 defines it purely via reward stagnation (_ε_) over a fixed iteration count. There is no explanation of how _ε_ relates to a prediction error bound, nor is 'high-fidelity prediction' operationalized anywhere. I verified whether _ε_ was defined as a validation error margin, but it is only described as an 'acceptable performance margin.' This leaves the theoretical motivation for the cutoff ambiguous: is it preventing overfitting, ensuring reward convergence, or bounding model error? The disconnect between the stated intent and the implemented heuristic needs clarification.

comments.title: Overstatement of Shapley Value Methodology
comments.quote: The proposed framework adopts Action Shapley which introduces a formulation for the problem of equitable training action valuation in reinforcement learning. ... A shapley technique is useful for action valuation because it satisfies nullity, symmetry, and linearity [45].
comments.explanation: The authors repeatedly claim the framework 'adopts Action Shapley' and evaluates actions based on Shapley axioms. However, Algorithms 1 and 2 rely on threshold-based reward comparison and single-action removal (ablation), not on computing marginal contributions over all coalitions or permutations. Ablation-based importance scoring does not inherently satisfy the Shapley axioms (e.g., symmetry or additivity) without careful weighting and averaging procedures. The methodology is effectively a greedy/ablation heuristic, not a game-theoretic valuation. While the Shapley connection provides a compelling narrative, the actual algorithmic procedure does not compute or approximate Shapley values, making the axiomatic claims unsupported and the 'Shapley-inspired' label misleading.

zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.label: Zero Shot (glm-4.7-flash)
zero_shot__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: The paper presents an original approach to RL action space evaluation using Shapley-inspired methods, demonstrating potential utility in resource management via a case study that effectively categorizes EC2 instance types. However, the work is hindered by a critical logical inconsistency in the description of Algorithm 1, where the iteration direction (descending) contradicts the update rule for determining the cut-off cardinality, making the methodology logically incoherent as written. Additionally, the case study contains a technical ambiguity regarding the feasibility of the recommended actions, as the points plotted in Figure 4(a) do not correspond to the discrete EC2 instance types used for training or execution.
